content.text: Anthropic Official Documentation

The official Anthropic documentation is the authoritative source for every API behavior, model capability, and platform feature. Unlike blog posts or third-party tutorials, the docs are kept current with every model and SDK release. This sub-subtopic teaches you how the documentation is structured, which sections to use for which tasks, and how to extract precise information efficiently.
Documentation Sections and When to Use Each
The documentation is organized into five main sections, each serving a different purpose in your development workflow:
Section
Purpose
Use When
Get Started
Quickstart guide, SDK installation, first API call
Starting a new project or onboarding a new team member
Build with Claude
Concept guides for each major API feature
Learning how a feature works before writing code
Models
Model catalog with capability comparison and pricing
Choosing a model or checking context window limits
API Reference
Exact parameter names, types, defaults, and constraints
Writing or debugging API calls
Admin API
Workspace, key, and user management endpoints
Enterprise provisioning and governance automation

The concept guides in 'Build with Claude' and the API reference are complementary, not interchangeable. The concept guide explains why and when to use a feature; the API reference explains exactly what to pass and what you get back. Read both before implementing anything non-trivial.
Navigating the Get Started Section

The Quickstart guide in the Get Started section walks through the minimal code needed to make a working API call. It is intentionally minimal — its job is to get you a successful response in under 10 minutes:

# The canonical first Claude API call from the Quickstart
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()  # reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from environment

message = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-8",
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=[
        {"role": "user", "content": "Hello, Claude"}
    ]
)

print(message.content)
# [TextBlock(text="Hello! How can I help you today?", type='text')]

The SDK section covers installation, version pinning, and proxy/timeout configuration for Python and TypeScript. If your environment has unusual network requirements (corporate proxy, custom CA bundle), the SDK section is where the relevant configuration options are documented.
SDK Version Pinning
Anthropic updates the SDK regularly. Pin the SDK version in your project to avoid silent behavior changes from transitive dependency updates:

# Python — pin to a minor version
pip install "anthropic>=0.34,<0.35"

# Or in requirements.txt
anthropic==0.34.2

# TypeScript — pin in package.json
npm install @anthropic-ai/sdk@0.26.0
Check the SDK changelog (the GitHub releases page for each SDK) when upgrading. Breaking changes are always documented there and in the official docs migration guide.
The Build with Claude Guides

The Build with Claude section is the most detailed and the most frequently needed. Each major API feature has its own guide with conceptual explanation, code examples, and common pitfalls. The key guides:
Messages API

The Messages API guide explains the request/response shape, streaming, token counting, and stop conditions. Critically, it documents the behavior of multi-turn conversations — how to build a message list that represents back-and-forth context — which is the source of most beginner bugs:

# Correct: append assistant turn and new user turn for multi-turn
messages = []

def chat(user_input: str) -> str:
    messages.append({"role": "user", "content": user_input})
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=messages
    )
    assistant_text = response.content[0].text
    messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": assistant_text})
    return assistant_text

print(chat("My name is Ada."))
print(chat("What is my name?"))  # Claude remembers "Ada" from previous turn
Tool Use

The Tool Use guide is among the most complex in the docs. It covers tool definition, the tool_use content block in Claude's response, the tool_result block you must send back, and multi-step tool loops. The guide includes a complete end-to-end example — read it in full before writing your own tool use code. The most common mistake is not handling the stop_reason correctly:
Vision

The Vision guide explains how to send images and PDFs as content blocks. The key decision documented there is base64 vs URL source — base64 is always reliable; URL sources depend on Claude's servers being able to fetch the URL, which fails for private or localhost URLs. For production, always encode images to base64:
Extended Thinking
The Extended Thinking guide covers the
thinking
parameter, budget token allocation, and the returned
thinking

content: content blocks. One critical constraint documented there: streaming and extended thinking require different handling — the thinking blocks arrive before the text blocks in the stream, and the guide shows how to separate them.

content.text: Prompt Caching
The Prompt Caching guide explains the
cache_control

breakpoint mechanism. Caching reduces cost and latency for repeated large prompts (system prompts, document context). The guide specifies the minimum cacheable size (1024 tokens for Haiku, 2048 for Sonnet/Opus) and how cache TTL works (5 minutes by default, 1 hour for extended cache). Cache hits are reflected in the
usage
object in the response:

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    max_tokens=1024,
    system=[{
        "type": "text",
        "text": very_long_system_prompt,  # must be >= 1024 tokens for Haiku
        "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}
    }],
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_question}]
)

usage = response.usage
print(f"Input tokens: {usage.input_tokens}")
print(f"Cache creation tokens: {usage.cache_creation_input_tokens}")
print(f"Cache read tokens: {usage.cache_read_input_tokens}")
# cache_read_input_tokens > 0 means the cache was hit
The API Reference
The API Reference is a machine-readable specification of every endpoint, parameter, and response field. Use it to answer questions that the concept guides leave implicit:
exact
temperature

content: ? (0.0 for deterministic, 1.0 is the max for most models)

content.text: max_tokens

content: What content block types can appear in a response? (text, tool_use, thinking)

content.text: stop_reason: 'max_tokens'
'end_turn'
The reference documents each field with its type, constraints, and a description of its effect. For enum fields like
stop_reason
, the reference lists every possible value and what triggers it. Always check the reference when you are uncertain about a field rather than inferring from examples.
The Model Catalog
The Models section maintains the current model catalog with capability comparisons. Use it for three decisions:
Decision
What to Check in the Catalog
Which model to use
Capability tier table — Haiku for speed/cost, Sonnet for balance, Opus for highest quality
Context window limits
Max input/output tokens for each model
Feature support
Which models support extended thinking, vision, tool use, prompt caching
The catalog also documents model version naming conventions. The pattern is
claude-<family>-<generation>-<date>
claude-haiku-4-5-20251001
). Use fully-specified model IDs in production code — never aliases like
"claude-3-haiku"
which may silently redirect to a different version.
The Changelog

The changelog is under the Documentation section and lists every API, model, and platform change with a date. Reading the most recent 3–4 entries before starting a new integration project takes two minutes and prevents building against a pattern that was superseded last week.
Items in the changelog fall into three categories:
New capabilities:
New API parameters, new model features, new endpoint. These expand what you can build.
Behavior changes:
Changes to how an existing feature works, often with a migration guide. These can break existing code if you upgrade the SDK.
New models:
New model IDs available. Check pricing and capability notes — a new model in the same family is not always a strict upgrade on every dimension.
Subscribe to the Anthropic developer newsletter for a weekly digest of changelog items. This is more reliable than manually checking the changelog and ensures you do not miss a deprecation notice.
Filing Documentation Issues and Feedback

Anthropic's documentation includes a feedback mechanism on each page. Use it when you find an error, an outdated example, or a section that is unclear. The feedback goes directly to the docs team and is the fastest way to get a correction merged.

For questions that are not documentation bugs — questions about behavior you cannot explain, edge cases, or integration problems — the right channel is the Anthropic Discord or the developer forum, not the feedback widget. Discord gives you a community of practitioners in addition to Anthropic staff.

# When filing a documentation issue, include:
# 1. The exact URL of the page
# 2. The section heading nearest to the error
# 3. What the doc says vs what the API actually does
# 4. The SDK version and language you were using

# Example report structure:
# Page: /docs/tool-use
# Section: "Handling tool_use stop_reason"
# Issue: The example passes tool_results as a list but the API requires
#         tool_results to be the content of the user message, not a
#         separate field. The example on line 34 causes a 400 error.
# SDK: anthropic-python 0.34.2
Searching the Documentation Effectively
The search bar in the documentation supports keyword and phrase search across all sections. Three search strategies that save time:
Search by parameter name:
If you see an unfamiliar field in an API response (e.g.,
cache_creation_input_tokens
), search for it verbatim. The search index includes every parameter name mentioned in the docs.
Search by error message:
Copy the error message string (not the HTTP status code) into the search. Many common errors have dedicated explanation pages.
Search by feature name:
Features have consistent naming across the docs, changelog, and release notes. Searching
tool_use
returns the concept guide, the API reference entry, and all changelog entries that mention it.

The documentation does not have a comprehensive FAQ page, but the Discord pinned messages in the #api-and-sdk channel function as one. Before opening a support ticket, check Discord first — the same question has usually been asked and answered within the last 30 days.
Documentation for Error Handling

The error handling section of the API reference is frequently overlooked but essential for production integrations. It documents every HTTP status code you can receive, what triggers it, and how to handle it:
HTTP Status
Error Type
Cause & Handling
invalid_request_error
Malformed request body — check parameter names and types
authentication_error
Missing or invalid API key — check ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env var
permission_error
API key lacks permission for this operation — check key scopes
not_found_error
Model ID does not exist — check the model catalog for valid IDs
invalid_request_error
Semantically invalid request — common for malformed tool definitions
rate_limit_error
Too many requests — implement exponential backoff
api_error
Anthropic server error — retry with backoff; report if persistent
overloaded_error
API temporarily overloaded — retry with longer backoff
The error documentation covers the structure of the error response body, including
error.type
and
error.message
fields. Always log both in production —
error.type
is stable across versions and suitable for programmatic error handling;
error.message
is human-readable and may change between releases.
Rate Limit and Usage Documentation

The rate limits section documents the default limits for each tier (Free, Build, Scale) across three dimensions: requests per minute (RPM), tokens per minute (TPM), and tokens per day (TPD). Key facts from the docs:
Rate limits are per-model, not per-account. Heavy Opus usage does not consume your Haiku quota.
x-ratelimit-limit-requests
and
x-ratelimit-remaining-requests
are returned in every response header. Read these headers rather than counting requests in your own code.
Upgrading to a higher tier requires contacting Anthropic through the console. Limits are not automatically raised based on usage.
Batch API requests count toward TPD but not RPM or TPM, making it useful for large workloads that are not time-sensitive.

# Read rate limit headers from the raw response
response = client.messages.with_raw_response.create(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    max_tokens=512,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "ping"}]
)

message = response.parse()
print(f"Remaining RPM: {response.headers.get('x-ratelimit-remaining-requests')}")
print(f"Remaining TPM: {response.headers.get('x-ratelimit-remaining-tokens')}")
print(f"Reset at: {response.headers.get('x-ratelimit-reset-requests')}")
Documentation for the Batch API

The Batch API is documented under the Build with Claude section. It allows submitting up to 10,000 requests in a single batch job that completes asynchronously within 24 hours at 50% reduced cost. The docs cover the full lifecycle:
Submit a batch with a list of request objects, each with a custom
custom_id
string.
Poll the batch status (or use a webhook) until the status is
ended
Download results from the results file URL in the batch object.

The Batch API is the correct choice for any workload that processes more than a few hundred items: bulk document classification, dataset annotation, large-scale prompt evaluation. The 50% cost reduction and the elimination of rate limit pressure make it strictly better than sequential requests for non-latency-sensitive tasks.
Summary

Anthropic's official documentation is organized into five sections: Get Started (quickstart and SDK setup), Build with Claude (concept guides for each major feature), Models (catalog and capability comparisons), API Reference (exact parameter specifications), and Admin API (enterprise management). Use the concept guides to understand a feature before implementing it; use the API reference for exact parameter details; read the changelog before starting any project. Pin SDK versions, always use base64 for image inputs, and file feedback directly on each doc page when you find an error.
